request.question: The 2010 Lars von Triers film that won awards for Best Danish Film, Best Actor, and Best Actress was filmed in which two countries?

title: 57th Bodil Awards
Peter Schønau Fog
65th Bodil Awards
63rd Bodil Awards
Sven Methling
50th Bodil Awards
58th Bodil Awards
Antichrist (film)
56th Bodil Awards
64th Bodil Awards
sentences: The 58th Bodil Awards were held on 7 March 2004 in Imperial Cinema in Copenhagen, Denmark, honouring the best national and foreign films of 2003.
Peter Mygind og Mette Horn hosted the event.

Lars von Trier's "Dogville" won the award for Best Danish Film while "The Inheritance" won the awards for best actor in leading and supporting roles and "Lykkevej" won the awards for best actress in leading and supporting roles.
Peter Schønau Fog (born 20 April 1971) is a Danish film director.
Fog's debut feature film, "Kunsten at græde i kor" (English: "The Art of Crying"), based on Erling Jepsen's novel of the same name, premiered 27 April 2007 and received critical acclaim.
For the film, he received the Nordic Council Film Prize for Best Film of 2007 as well as the 2007 Robert Award for Best Danish Film and 2008 Bodil Award for Best Danish Film.
The 65th Bodil Awards were held on 3 March 2012 in the Bremen Theater in Copenhagen, Denmark, honouring the best national and foreign films of 2010.
Lars von Trier's "Melancholia" won the awards for Best Danish Film and Best Cinematography (Alberto Claro).

The only other multiple winner was "A Funny Man", which took the awards for Best Actor (Nikolaj Lie Kaas), Best Supporting Actor (Lars Ranthe) and Best Set Design (Charlotte Bay Garnov and Peter Grant).
It also won the new Audience Award which was introduced this year in collaboration with Blockbuster.

The awards for Best Leading and Supporting Actresses went to Lena Maria Christensen won the award for Best Actress for her performance in "A Family" and Paprika Steen won Best Supporting Actress for "SuperClásico". "
Testamentet" directed by Christian Sønderby Jepsen earned the award for Best Documentary. "
Winter's Bone" was named Best American Film while the Iranian "A Separation" was selected as Best Non-American Film.
The 63rd Bodil Awardss were held on 21 March 2010 in the Imperial Cinema in Copenhagen, Denmark, honouring the best national and foreign films of 2009.
Lasse Rimmer hosted the event.

Lars von Triers "Antichrist" was the big winner, receiving both the awards for Best Danish Film, Best Actor (Willem Dafoe), Best Actress (Charlotte Gainsbourg), Best Cinematographer (Anthony Dod Mantle) and a Special Award to Eidnes Andersen for sound design. "

Deliver Us from Evil" won both the awards for Best Supporting Actor () and Best Supporting Actress () while "Headhunter (2009 film)" which had come to the ceremony with the most nominations, five in three categories, left empty-handed.
The documentary "The Invisible Cell" about The Blekinge Street Gang won the award for Best Documentary.
Carsten Myllerup, Linda Krogsøe Holmberg and Jens Mikkelsen received a Bodil Honorary Award for their role in the foundation of the alternative film school Super16.
Sven Johan Methling (or Sven Methling Jr.) (1918–2005) was a Danish film director and screenwriter.
The son of actor and film director Svend Methling, Methling Jr. was best known for a series of light-hearted comedies.
His 1959 comedy "Vi er allesammen tossede" ("All of us are Crazy") received the Bodil Award for Best Danish Film.
Methling also received the Bodil Award for Best Danish Film for the 1955 war drama "Der kom en dag" ("There Will Come a Day").
The 50th Bodil Awards ceremony was held in 1997 in Copenhagen, Denmark, honouring the best national and foreign films of 1996.
Lars von Trier's "Breaking the Waves" won the award for Best Danish Film and Emily Watson and Katrin Cartlidge  won the awards for best leading and supporting actresses.
Max von Sydow for his role in "Hamsun" and Zlatko Buric won the award for best supporting actor for his role in "Pusher".
Bodil Kjær, one of the two film people named Bodil for whom the statuette is named, the other being Bodil Ipsen, reveived an Bodil Honorary Award, bringing her total number of Bodil wins up to four.
The 58th Bodil Awards were held on 27 February 2005 in Imperial Cinema in Copenhagen, Denmark, honouring the best national and foreign films of 2004.
Nikolaj Arcel's debut film "King's Game" and Susanne Bier's "Brothers" were both nominated for five awards.
"King's Game" won the awards for Best Danish Film and Best Actor in a Supporting Role (Søren Pilmark) while Connie Nielsen won the award for Best Actress for her performance in "Brothers".
Mads Mikkelsen won the award for Best Actor for his performance in "Pusher II"
Antichrist is a 2009 English-language Danish experimental horror film written and directed by Lars von Trier, and starring Willem Dafoe and Charlotte Gainsbourg.

It tells the story of a couple who, after the death of their child, retreat to a cabin in the woods where the man experiences strange visions and the woman manifests increasingly violent sexual behaviour and sadomasochism.
The narrative is divided into a prologue, four chapters and an epilogue.
The film was primarily a Danish production and co-produced by companies from six different European countries.
It was filmed in Germany and Sweden.
The 56th Bodil Awards were held on 2 March 2003 in the Imperial Cinema in Copenhagen, Denmark, honouring the best national and foreign films of 2002.
Susanne Bier's "Open Hearts" took three awards, winning Best Danish Film as well as the awards for Best leading Actor Actress which went to Nikolaj Lie Kaas and Paprika Steen respectively.
Paprika Steen also won the award for Best Actress in a Leading Role for her performance in Okay, while Jens Albinus won Best Actor in a Leading Role.
The David Lynch film "Mulholland Drive" was named Best American Film and Almodovar's "Talk to Her" the Best Non-American Film.
Kim Fupz Aakeson, Anders Thomas Jensen and Mogens Rukov collectively received a Bodil Honorary Award for their work as screenwriters.
The 64th Bodil Awards were held on 20 February 2011 at the Bremen Theater in Copenhagen, Denmark, honouring the best national and foreign films of 2010.
Ditte Hansen and Louise Mieritz hosted the event.
"Submarino" had most nominations, with five, but the ceremony did not have a clear winner. "
R" won the award for Best Danish Film and its protagonist, Pilou Asbæk, won the award for Best Actor while Trine Dyrholm was named Best Actress ("In a Better World").
Kurt Ravn ("Nothing's All Bad") and Patricia Schumann ("Submarino") won the awards for Best Supporting Actor and Actress. "
Armadillo" won both the awards for Best Documentary and Best Cinematographer.
Tobias Lindholm received a Special Award for his contribution as a screenwriter both to "R" and "Submarino".
Henning Moritzen was given a Bodil Honorary Award for his contribution to Danish film.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

Lars von Trier's "Melancholia" won the awards for Best Danish Film and Best Cinematography (Alberto Claro).  The only other multiple winner was "A Funny Man", which took the awards for Best Actor (Nikolaj Lie Kaas), Best Supporting Actor (Lars Ranthe) and Best Set Design (Charlotte Bay Garnov and Peter Grant). The awards for Best Leading and Supporting Actresses went to Lena Maria Christensen won the award for Best Actress for her performance in "A Family" and Paprika Steen won Best Supporting Actress for "SuperClásico". " Testamentet" directed by Christian Sønderby Jepsen earned the award for Best Documentary. " Winter's Bone" was named Best American Film while the Iranian "A Separation" was selected as Best Non-American Film.

57th Bodil Awards | The 58th Bodil Awards were held on 7 March 2004 in Imperial Cinema in Copenhagen, Denmark, honouring the best national and foreign films of 2003.  Peter Mygind og Mette Horn hosted the event.  Lars von Trier's "Dogville" won the award for Best Danish Film while "The Inheritance" won the awards for best actor in leading and supporting roles and "Lykkevej" won the awards for best actress in leading and supporting roles.

Lars von Trier's "Breaking the Waves" won the award for Best Danish Film and Emily Watson and Katrin Cartlidge  won the awards for best leading and supporting actresses.  Max von Sydow for his role in "Hamsun" and Zlatko Buric won the award for best supporting actor for his role in "Pusher".  Bodil Kjær, one of the two film people named Bodil for whom the statuette is named, the other being Bodil Ipsen, reveived an Bodil Honorary Award, bringing her total number of Bodil wins up to four.

63rd Bodil Awards | The 63rd Bodil Awardss were held on 21 March 2010 in the Imperial Cinema in Copenhagen, Denmark, honouring the best national and foreign films of 2009.  Lasse Rimmer hosted the event.  Lars von Triers "Antichrist" was the big winner, receiving both the awards for Best Danish Film, Best Actor (Willem Dafoe), Best Actress (Charlotte Gainsbourg), Best Cinematographer (Anthony Dod Mantle) and a Special Award to Eidnes Andersen for sound design. " Deliver Us from Evil" won both the awards for Best Supporting Actor () and Best Supporting Actress () while "Headhunter (2009 film)" which had come to the ceremony with the most nominations, five in three categories, left empty-handed.  The documentary "The Invisible Cell" about The Blekinge Street Gang won the award for Best Documentary.  Carsten Myllerup, Linda Krogsøe Holmberg and Jens Mikkelsen received a Bodil Honorary Award for their role in the foundation of the alternative film school Super16.

Michael Flessas | Michael C. Flessas (born June 2, 1959 in Miami, Florida), is the birth name of American actor Michael Flessas, who is of Greek ancestry.  Flessas' most notable film role was "Angry Man" in the Cannes Film Festival 2000 Palme d'Or winning film "Dancer in the Dark" directed by Danish film director Lars von Trier.  Originally, the director himself considered playing the role but, instead, the role was given to Flessas.  "Dancer in the Dark" starred Icelandic singer/actress Björk who won the Best Actress award at Cannes for her role.  French film icon, César Award winner, and Academy Award nominee Catherine Deneuve, and other noteworthy artists such as Academy Award and Tony Award winner Joel Grey, Peter Stormare, David Morse, and Stellan Skarsgård also performed in the multiple prize winning film.  One of Björk's songs for the film received an Academy Award nomination for Best Song.

Emily Watson | Emily Margaret Watson, OBE (born 14 January 1967) is an English actress.  She was nominated for the Academy Award for Best Actress for her debut film role as Bess McNeil in Lars von Trier's "Breaking the Waves" (1996) and for her role as Jacqueline du Pré in "Hilary and Jackie" (1998), winning the BIFA Award for Best Actress for the latter.  She won the BAFTA TV Award for Best Actress for playing Janet Leach in the 2011 ITV television biopic "Appropriate Adult".

64th Bodil Awards | The 64th Bodil Awards were held on 20 February 2011 at the Bremen Theater in Copenhagen, Denmark, honouring the best national and foreign films of 2010.  Ditte Hansen and Louise Mieritz hosted the event.  "Submarino" had most nominations, with five, but the ceremony did not have a clear winner. " R" won the award for Best Danish Film and its protagonist, Pilou Asbæk, won the award for Best Actor while Trine Dyrholm was named Best Actress ("In a Better World").  Kurt Ravn ("Nothing's All Bad") and Patricia Schumann ("Submarino") won the awards for Best Supporting Actor and Actress. " Armadillo" won both the awards for Best Documentary and Best Cinematographer.  Tobias Lindholm received a Special Award for his contribution as a screenwriter both to "R" and "Submarino".  Henning Moritzen was given a Bodil Honorary Award for his contribution to Danish film.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
1) Evidence found:
- "Melancholia" is attributed to Lars von Trier and is said to have won Best Danish Film: "Lars von Trier's 'Melancholia' won the awards for Best Danish Film" (passage [1]).
- "Antichrist" (Lars von Trier) is listed as a big winner of Best Danish Film, Best Actor (Willem Dafoe), and Best Actress (Charlotte Gainsbourg): "'Antichrist' ... Best Danish Film, Best Actor, Best Actress" (passage [4]).
- For films of 2010, the 64th Bodil entry shows "'R' won the award for Best Danish Film" and lists Pilou Asbæk (Best Actor) and Trine Dyrholm (Best Actress for "In a Better World") separately (passage [7]).

2) Ambiguities:
- Multiple candidate film titles appear: "Melancholia" (passage [1]) and "Antichrist" (passage [4]) are both Lars von Trier films associated with Best Danish Film; only "Antichrist" in the passages is explicitly tied to Best Actor and Best Actress together. The passages do not state a single 2010 Lars von Trier film winning all three awards. Also the 2010-winner list (passage [7]) names "R" as Best Danish Film, not a Lars von Trier film.

3) Bridge entity / Missing fact:
- Bridge entity(ies) to resolve next hop: the film page(s) "Melancholia" and "Antichrist" (as they appear in the passages). 
- Precise missing fact needed: the filming locations — specifically, the two countries in which the (target) film was filmed.

4) Retrieval direction (prioritized):
- Open "Melancholia" (film) page — check lede/infobox and "Filming" or "Production" sections for "Filmed in", "Locations", or "Country" entries. Search keywords: Melancholia filming locations; "Melancholia (film) infobox filming locations".
- Open "Antichrist" (film) page — check lede/infobox and "Filming" / "Production" sections to confirm filming countries (since passage [4] links it to the three awards). Search keywords: Antichrist filming locations; "Antichrist (film) filming".
- If needed, open "64th Bodil Awards" and "65th Bodil Awards" pages (the award-year pages cited) to confirm which film corresponds to the 2010 awards year and to verify which film is the intended target.
- If the film title remains ambiguous, fetch each candidate film's page and compare their "Filming locations" sections.

Final note: I defer the final answer until those film pages/sections are retrieved and their filming-location details are confirmed.
hop2_docs: Antichrist (film) | Antichrist is a 2009 English-language Danish experimental horror film written and directed by Lars von Trier, and starring Willem Dafoe and Charlotte Gainsbourg.  It tells the story of a couple who, after the death of their child, retreat to a cabin in the woods where the man experiences strange visions and the woman manifests increasingly violent sexual behaviour and sadomasochism.  The narrative is divided into a prologue, four chapters and an epilogue.  The film was primarily a Danish production and co-produced by companies from six different European countries.  It was filmed in Germany and Sweden.
The Antichrist (film) | The Antichrist (Italian: "L'anticristo; also known as The Tempter" ) is a 1974 Italian horror film directed by Alberto De Martino.

Satanic film | A Satanic film is a subgenre of horror film, and at times other film genres, that involves the Devil as a concept or a character.  Common themes/characters in Satanic film include the Antichrist, demonic possession, exorcism, and witchcraft.

Alberto De Martino | Alberto De Martino (12 June 1929 – 2 June 2015) was an Italian film director and screenwriter.  Born in Rome, De Martino started as a child actor and later returned to the cinema where worked as a screenwriter, director and dubbing supervisor.  De Martino's films as a director specialised in wellcrafted knock-offs of Hollywood hit films.  These films were specifically created films in Western, horror and mythology genres which were developed for the international market. " The Telegraph" stated that his best known of these film was probably "The Antichrist".  "The Antichrist" capitalized on the box-office appeal of "The Exorcist" (1973) and in its first week in the United States earned a greater box office than "Jaws".  Horror film fans also remember him for "The Blancheville Monster", "Blood Link", "Formula for a Murder", "Medusa vs the Son of Hercules" and "Holocaust 2000".

The Prodigal Planet | The Prodigal Planet is a 1983 film by Russell S. Doughten and directed by Donald W. Thompson.  It is the fourth in a series of at least five films (one still in production) based on an evangelical interpretation of Bible prophecy and the rise of the Antichrist.  Unlike the other films in the series that were filmed in Iowa, this one was filmed in New Mexico.

63rd Bodil Awards | The 63rd Bodil Awardss were held on 21 March 2010 in the Imperial Cinema in Copenhagen, Denmark, honouring the best national and foreign films of 2009.  Lasse Rimmer hosted the event.  Lars von Triers "Antichrist" was the big winner, receiving both the awards for Best Danish Film, Best Actor (Willem Dafoe), Best Actress (Charlotte Gainsbourg), Best Cinematographer (Anthony Dod Mantle) and a Special Award to Eidnes Andersen for sound design. " Deliver Us from Evil" won both the awards for Best Supporting Actor () and Best Supporting Actress () while "Headhunter (2009 film)" which had come to the ceremony with the most nominations, five in three categories, left empty-handed.  The documentary "The Invisible Cell" about The Blekinge Street Gang won the award for Best Documentary.  Carsten Myllerup, Linda Krogsøe Holmberg and Jens Mikkelsen received a Bodil Honorary Award for their role in the foundation of the alternative film school Super16.

Image of the Beast (film) | Image of the Beast is a 1981 film by Russell S. Doughten and directed by Donald M. Thompson.  It is part of a series of four films popular in evangelical Christian groups, dealing with the subject of bible prophecy.  The film is an account of the rise of the antichrist, who establishes a world government and forces everybody to take the mark of the beast.  A handful of Christians refuse to take the mark and try to survive living apart from society.
response.summary_2: Antichrist was filmed in Germany and Sweden. Antichrist is a 2009 English-language Danish experimental horror film.
